pr_review_comment_body: Why is this method moved? It makes  it harder to review the changes, IMHO.

These are custom doxygen CLI aliases.  `@ca` is for "command argument" (e.g., in `* @cparam channel manager interval @ca{interval-seconds}`).

Feel free to check this link for more info: https://openthread.io/reference/cli/contribute?hl=nl.

> Including the deprecated ones?

Yes. Some still use them so we can't omit them.

> Can we do this without using an additional file?

It could be done but I'd rather not.
cibuildwheel is more than just a CI tool & as such, I see its config file living at project root just like the ones from flake8/isort/coverage.
It's more & more common for cibuildwheel users to put there config in `pyproject.toml` and be able to just run `cibuildwheel`, whatever the CI provider, or run locally (which I use extensively) to reproduce the steps that would happen on CI.
Running locally, I can just do `cibuildwheel --platform macos --config-file cibuildwheel.toml` rather than remembering/typing all of the CI config from the GitHub workflow in my console.
This file is CI provider independent so if it were to be kept & moved, would you be okay to move it to `.ci/` ?
Another option if you're open to it would be to add a `pyproject.toml` file with cibuildwheel config inside. It would simplify the workflow even more (& running locally) by removing the need to specify a config file:
```toml
[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=43"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[tool.cibuildwheel]
skip = ["pp*", "*-musllinux*"]
test-extras = "test"
test-command = [
    "PYTHONWARNINGS=always PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 PSUTIL_DEBUG=1 python {project}/psutil/tests/runner.py",
    "PYTHONWARNINGS=always PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 PSUTIL_DEBUG=1 python {project}/psutil/tests/test_memleaks.py"
]

[tool.cibuildwheel.macos]
archs = ["x86_64", "arm64"]
```
Not worth a CI round though. Feel free to merge as-is.

> Any strong argument against dictionary ?

A dict would duplicate the information we already have in the names of the attributes (`ch_A`) in the keys of the dict.
The stated reason for why we need the collections at instance level (iirc) was to iterate through them, which a list fulfils.

> If you still want to see the renames introduced, you're free to open another PR for it, or perhaps an issue first, so that we can discuss whether we even want this rename

What is the sense to return old and different names? This situation fully looks like:

```python
# was
class FirstAnswer:
    online_num: int
    max_online: int
    status_code: int
    description: str

class OnlineFieldInSecondAnswer:
    current: int
    max: int

class SecondAnswer:
    online: OnlineFieldInSecondAnswer
    status_code_number: int
    custom_info: list

# now
class OnlineInAnswer:
    current: int
    max: int

class AbstractAnswer(ABC)
    online: OnlineInAnswer
    status_code: int

class FirstAnswer(AbstractAnswer):
    description: str

class SecondAnswer(AbstractAnswer):
    custom_info: list
```

See? I renamed fields to **one** and **common** names. Do you really want to see inside this code something like:

```python
class FirstAnswer(AbstractAnswer):
    description: str

    @property
    def online_num(self):
        return self.online.current

    @property
    def max_online(self):
        return self.online.max

class SecondAnswer(AbstractAnswer):
    custom_info: list

    @property
    def status_code_number(self):
        return self.status_code
```

Now do you see how is dirty code after returning old names, just as useless aliases? Also note that this example only uses 2 classes and 4 fields in each. I have much larger numbers, so the code size will increase at a very high rate.

In almost all cases `tbb::mutex` should be considered because it is not expected pathetically degrade like `tbb::spin_mutex` with heavy contention (even with hundreds of threads). While `tbb::spin_mutex` has several ticks less overhead that might be useful in non-contented cases (e.g. when only few threads work with this mutex), however, I do not know any cases when `tbb::spin_mutex` is better than `tbb::mutex`. I think we can consider deprecation of `tbb::spin_mutex`.
As for proposed optimization, it is applied in [`tbb::mutex`](https://github.com/oneapi-src/oneTBB/blob/master/include/oneapi/tbb/mutex.h#L58-L73) and I would remain `tbb::spin_mutex` as is not to increase its overhead (and not to lose its last advantage if any).

>  "inner" or "code" targets that own files (python_library, python_tests, files, resources), and then "artifact" targets (python_distribution, pex_binary) that don't own files but instead represent an artifact to be built. We don't model this distinction explicitly, but we probably should. Maybe we should even use different terms for them.

This is precisely why I have been so excited about that cleanup a few months ago for `python_awslambda` and `pex_binary` to no longer have `sources`. This is the mental model I've been using since that breakthrough, including when envisioning the elusive "re-envisioning targets" proposal. I do think there's a big difference between those two types of targets, and it is valuable to codify it more.

Still handwavy, but imo: targets work really well for artifact-type targets like `pex_binary`. They also seem to work well for 3rd-party deps metadata. They do not work well for first-party code, with issues like it being too hard to update just 1 file's metadata. So, I could envision us codifying this distinction by replacing 1st-party targets w/ something else entirely, for example.

Upon review of the general `pickle` API, I decided to stick with `__reduce__` as opposed to `__getstate__` and `__setstate__`, the reason being that implementing `__setstate__` would involve reapplying the state of an `asn1crypto` object _after_ it has been created. This is more complicated than it sounds, because `pickle` doesn't call `__init__` on object creation in typical circumstances. The `__setstate__` implementation would have to take that into account. IMHO that's more trouble than it's worth, and would potentially require a lot of extra tests to make sure all `asn1crypto` types are properly pickled and unpickled.

On the other hand, implementing `__reduce__`, while it involves a lower-level API, is much easier in this case: `__reduce__` allows the implementer to not only control object state, but also object creation, which allows us to get away with simply calling the relevant class's `.load(...)` method on the pickled data. Bonus: `.dump()` and `.load()` are already extensively tested.

Summary: I believe the `__reduce__` approach is much simpler and less error-prone in this particular situation. :)

> If we think to expose this as public API, we have to test it better, write docs etc.

Of course, but that has time until we actually expose this outside of `torchvision.prototype`.

> Yes, feature like extending resize to make it work on other types would be nice but then we have to ensure which scope we provide.

I don't think this is nice-to-have, but rather integral. Our transforms should be flexible enough to cater to the needs of 90% of our user base without them using hacky workarounds. To give an example, Franciscos new project already needs this if they want to adopt our new transforms.

> Can we register on other non-tensor types, e.g. `str`

Nope, not for now. The output wrapping into a `Feature` already implied this, but I will make it explicit with an error. This way, we have instant feedback.

> How about parity then ? If we provide publicly add operation (`dispatch.register`), then we may want to provide removal operation, e.g `dispatch.deregister`. I do not see any application of that at the moment...

I think that is fair game to wait for someone actually requesting this. In the current setup if you call `register` again with an already registered feature type, the old entry is simply overwritten. Thus, if the user wants to have a custom kernel this is already supported. 

I don't see a use case for de-registering feature types. If we had such a functionality, calling a dispatcher with a de-registered feature type would result in an error. Thus, it should be much simpler to not call the dispatcher in the first place with whatever feature type you want to de-register.

@dispatch
def resize(...):

@dispatch(

I agree its a lot but it seems necessary? 

Context -> Share state state between components

useMemo -> So we don't cause any of the components to re-render by creating a new hook every time the hover state changes

"Fake" Hook function -> Defining a hook function within a component/context is probably the easiest way to create a hook with shared state among multiple components. The alternative is to give all of the components a unique ID that they can use to make sure they all subscribe to the same state. That might actually look cleaner since now we wouldn't need to define a hook within a React component/context, however it means that we would need to use a WeakMap and ref objects that die with the component as keys to avoid keeping state around forever.

-> State -> We need the `hovered` state! 

I'm open to other approaches though this one just seemed to encapsulate everything most simply without needing to create an external store.

An else after a loop should tell the reader that there's control flow here. Returns are already control flow. The else is meaningless, it has no impact, but a reader has to read the code above and prove that - plus they also have to try to align the else with the for and not with the nested ifs. Plus they have to realize that the else does nothing. Many Python users don't even know it's valid to use an else with a for; there are special cases (with break) where it's really nice and solves a problem with a teachable structure instead of injecting loop control variables, which is why it's there, but it shouldn't be used if there's no break.

Eventually you'll probably see me argue against else's and elif's that don't have an effect in control flow either, but this one is easier due to the fact it's a rare and specialized feature for `for` and `break`.

So in at the moment whenever we check preconditions, we check them on a different block for parity and a different one for geth. It's the so called "checking_block" seen [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/98ae425a3f39c70d1a6272b8da9bca39bbda3b73/raiden/network/proxies/token_network.py#L223).

The checking block is defined below:

https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/98ae425a3f39c70d1a6272b8da9bca39bbda3b73/raiden/network/rpc/client.py#L839-L849

It's always `pending` in geth (this is how estimate gas works there) but `latest` in parity as in parity there can exist no pending block. Before you ask we can't configure geth to also be on latest. They don't follow the spec and don't allow a block identifier argument for estimate gas. Issue [here](https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/issues/2586).

The code before was waiting for the settlement block. And once it arrived it started the settle() call, which contains a gas estimation. But `estimate_gas` for settle on the latest block being the settle block number will fail (see the [contract code](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-contracts/blob/03024ac310f34b9d8f5ddd064413684416223845/raiden_contracts/data/source/raiden/TokenNetwork.sol#L697)). We did not see it in geth since it's estmating on the pending block (which is latest +1).

This is what it's supposed to do. LMT = Local Mean Time. I think you may be right that this is again a Version 1 vs. Version 2 issue. I believe the appropriate lower bound is something close to the negative equivalent of the epochalypse: `1901-12-13T20:45:52`.

If it works within that range (or even within a range starting ~24 hours after that), that is probably best.

1970-01-01 is also an *OK* lower bound in the sense that the TZ database doesn't make any guarantees about data quality before that, but I think that if we see any deviations in timestamps within the range `[-2**31, 2**31]` between `.astimezone()` and `tz.gettz()`, it may be because the two are using different data sources and not just different implementations reading the same data source; in that case, we may need to scrap the whole idea.

Since the metrics code is called a _lot_ in an application and I've seen it show up in performance profiling in the past, I was curious if this was the most efficient way to serialize the tags. Particularly since the standard wisdom is that f-strings and `str.join` are faster than `+` concatenation. The results were kinda surprising! 

https://gist.github.com/spladug/b936c5a9528ced0d1095a01aa1136075

So if we want this to be as quick as possible, something like the following would appear good:

```python
def format_tags_concatenate_fstrings(tags: Optional[Dict]) -> str:
    if not tags:                                                  
        return ""                                                 
                                                                  
    formatted_str = "|#"                                          
    for key, value in tags.items():                               
        formatted_str += f"{key}:{value},"                        
    return formatted_str[:-1]                                     
```

(it's a tiny bit faster without stripping the trailing comma off, but I think wasting bytes in our network packets is probably worse)

Feel free to play around with benchmarking and find a faster one too :)

You are definitely on the right track! It's not quite easy to understand, because there's a lot of different values.

Like you mention, there's indeed a different concept for `[1]` and `List[int]`. The latter is obviously a `class TypeAlias(LazyValueWrapper)`, which is itself a wrapper around the stub `builtins.list[int]` (which is I think a `GenericClass`, but I might be wrong). The actual object will probably be a `TreeInstance()` that takes the class as its argument. If you define something with literals there's a `class SequenceLiteralValue()` that wraps basically around the `TreeInstance` that I just described.

However note that this is all done by composition, not inheritance and it's lazy. This makes sense, especially historically (I might not do it exactly the same way if I would implement Jedi from the ground up). The lazyness was put there to avoid calculating the generics when it's not necessary. In Jedi `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5][3]` actually returns an `int` literal `4` without any generics calculation. `Sequence._get_wrapped_value` is never called, because we can just call `SequenceLiteralValue().py__simple_getitem__(4)`. However if we want to access `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5].pop(3)` it will return an `int` instance. This happens, because in that case the generics of the list literal is calculated and from there we just use typeshed to get the correct types.

So kind of a TL;DR; `List[int]` is represented as something like `TreeInstance(GenericClass(ClassValue(builtins.list), Generics<int>))`. `[1]` is represented as `
SequenceLiteralValue([1])`, which kind of lazily resolves to `SequenceLiteralValue(TreeInstance(GenericClass(ClassValue(builtins.list), Generics<int>))`. Yeah, this stuff is pretty crazy haha :).

I think this still kind of works like intended. BUT for tuples this is kind of an issue...

> I guess the bit I'm missing is the relationship between the two instance forms of tuples -- the ClassValue form and the Tuple form. [...]
> Is it because jedi needs a way to represent whether or not the tuple is homogeneous?

I just never thought about this case really. You make a very very good observation. The end result is that `tuple` and `Tuple` have different tree nodes. `Tuple` is a very special case in typeshed, because unlike `List`, `Dict`, `Set`, etc. It's **not** a `TypeAlias`, it's its own class.

Reading your stuff and thinking about it for a bit, we should probably do one of the following:
- Make the `Tuple().tree_node` use `tuple` instead of `Tuple`.
- Make `Sequence()._get_wrapped_value` somehow return a `Tuple`. However this would probably lead to strange cases where goto would land on `Tuple` instead of `tuple`. I'm not really sure how to do this exactly, because Tuple is kind of a weird wrapper around `tuple()` and itself not very flexible, but we could probably refactor that.
- Combine those two approaches and use tree_node `builtins.tuple()` and use a modified version of `Tuple` so we have a common ancestor in our composition chain.

I think the first proposal is probably the best and easiest (I'm not sure the other ones are even feasible). It would probably look like this:

```
class Tuple(BaseTypingValueWithGenerics):
    [...]
    @property                 
    def tree_node(self):      
        return self._wrapped_value.tree_node
```

Basically it [always copies on Python 2]( https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Modules/_io/bytesio.c#L798 ), but will [skip copying on Python 3]( https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.7/Modules/_io/bytesio.c#L923 ) if it's already a `bytes` object. So the idea here is if we already have a `bytes` object (e.g. reading from all(?) Zarr stores), then we should try to pass the `bytes` object through unchanged. If we coerce a copy in the process on Python 3, this is no worse as `BytesIO` would have copied it anyways if it wasn't a `bytes` object.

They commit to a chain tip when they announce their burn in the sliding window, and the commit to a descendant of that chain tip when they start their tenure. I just want to say a few words about _*args_, _**kwargs_. Many people disagree with me. So it's just my experience and my vision and nothing more.
In some cases using _args_, _kwargs_ is really beautiful and useful. For example _decorators_, _wrappers_ or something like this:

```
def example(*names):
    pass
def example2(name: str, *patterns):
    pass

@dataclass
class ExampleClass:
   # props...

ExampleClass(**props)
```

Maybe using _args_ / _kwargs_ is very **_PYTHONIC_** way. But overuse can make a project just unsupported, hard to understand / debug. Any function / method can turn into a 'black box' or magic. Let's imagine you are opening a new project and you see the class:

```
class Resources:
      def __init__(cpu: int, mem: int):
             pass
```

All is clear and you know how work with it. You can just open class and fast check if you forgot something. Now let's make a few changes(just imagine that you see it for the first time or second):

```
# this is not quite obvious
# Resources(1, 2, disk=100) ? Resources(1, 2, hard_drive=100) ? Resources(1, 2, usage=100)
class Resources:
      def __init__(cpu: int, mem: int, **kwargs):
             pass

# let's add more magic
class Resources(BaseResources):
       def __init__(*args, **kwargs):
             # just some code, calls etc...

# how to work with it?
# Resources(stones=5) ? Resources(water=True) etc.
# and now let's imagine that we have a lot of such classes and they are changing...
```

Yes, I can read docs or do debugging or something else, but I wouldn't waste time if it was more obvious(I mean just **cmd+p** in _PyCharm_ and I see everything).

This is an interesting example, I ran this code on `draw_cfg.py` and this is what it returned: At first, I got totally confused but then it made sense and this is how the CFG actually should look like. The reason I think that there is a path from the `end` block to every block in the CFG is because of how unreachable blocks are stored. These blocks are added as predecessors to blocks that are reachable and they are not successors to the `start` block (this makes them unreachable). However, if we traverse the CFG from the `end` to the `start` we will be doing so by visiting every ancestor (i.e predecessor) block of `end`. This means that reachable blocks will be visited as well as unreachable blocks.

I'm afraid we can't negate the `SETUPTOOLS_USE_DISTUTILS` option - there are actual bugfixes in setuptools that are not in the standard library (and never will be).

I also wouldn't want to mess with anything that might be used in an existing build, and my experience of `setup.py` files is that could be _literally anything_. "Redirecting" the user profile[^1] is one such thing that could break builds for unrelated reasons.

I tried a lot of ways to pass the required arguments into setuptools, and this is by far the safest way that doesn't rely on invoking `setup.py` directly. Personally that's how I set up my own builds, but it's not a good option for cibuildwheel.

[^1]: It's actually just pretending that it's been redirected, and using the leakiest possible way to do so. Really not a good idea.

It seems reasonable to assume that in general we expect this to be a fixed list / static property of the plugin, rather than something dynamically determined.  This would allow us to defer loading of the plugin until we encounter an experiment that actually needs it, which could help the backend start up faster and use less memory in a world where there are many dynamic plugins and they're more heavyweight.  We probably don't get much advantage from it right now (where the summary-based plugins tend to be the lightweight ones anyway), and there are some downsides as well (if the plugin takes a while to load or has an import-time error, this impact occurs later on, which might be bad), but I wonder if it might be more future-proof that way.

Currently this function is independent of what value will be inserted to define the boundary between two pages. This function outputs a `param_name`, and the caller is responsible for inserting the desired value.

This flexibility comes at a cost: The caller is expected to insert a value that makes the filter stronger than any of the filters that were removed in its place. For example, if there's an existing `x > $val, where val = 5` filter, the parameterizer will remove it and in it's place put `x > $__paged_param_0`. The caller will probably use a value greater than `5` for the `__paged_param_0` parameter, since that is in the spec of the parameter generator.

However, it would be nice to assert that all filters that were removed are replaced by stronger filters. One way to do that is to pass the desired parameter value into this function. That will add a cross-dependency on parameter generation, but it's a dependency that's very easy to mock out in tests, so we can still test the modules separately.

The callback isn't intended to be a handler, and should really be limited to a minimal operation akin to setting or clearing a flag.

The specific use-case I had in mind for this is an `is_changed` property which is mapped to the `endpoint.{relation_name}.changed` flag, where clearing the flag would then automatically invoke `inst.is_changed = False` which would presumably update a hash value of relevant data in stored state so that the change is considered "handled." This is [handled automatically by the library using this feature](https://github.com/juju-solutions/ops-reactive-interface/pull/1/files#diff-be35484ccd89c06a5d619f4c4cbfc0aa1c541b7152f79f255b75178c566e12c5R121-R126) so that the interface APIs can just keep all charms.reactive-related code in a single `manage_flags` method (since that is guaranteed to only be invoked when used in a reactive charm) and otherwise assume that changes will be acknowledged via the property. Then, reactive charms can interact with the flag in the natural "reactive" fashion: have a handler triggered by the flag, handle the change, and clear the flag to indicate that it's done, without having to worry about forgetting to clear the property as well.

However, I'm thinking that this may be a bit too much magic behavior and maybe it would be safer in the end to just put a bit more onus on the reactive developer by saying that the `.changed` flag will be set automatically, but it's up to the charm to clear it and if the specific interface API also requires a property to be cleared, you'll have to remember to do that as well.

If you just call functions like `np.random.random()` from all over the place (i.e. separate tests), then you will get different numbers depending on test order execution or just the machine you're on.
Then you might get numbers and failing tests at arbitrary times, which is annoying.

You can call `np.random.seed(0)` each time before generating random numbers, or create a `rng = RandomState(seed)` and call the methods on that, or use `with NumpyRNGContext(seed)`. It seems in Astropy all of those patterns are used:
https://github.com/astropy/astropy/search?q=random&unscoped_q=random

Looks like in astropy-healpix we already have one "rogue" `np.random` call, that should be cleaned up as well:
https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/blob/528292bc2bff01563d6c696880020345cde88d40/astropy_healpix/tests/test_healpy.py#L285

IMO the best solution is to not use random numbers at all in almost all cases. If you write an explicit list of numbers as test cases and manually choose 1 - 10 numbers well to cover what you want to test, it's just better than producing random numbers, becuase it's more explicit and you know what is being tested, e.g. which range of numbers.

>  When sorting this result, what is the sort key? Are they sorted alphabetically?

According to [docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.listdir),  the list is in arbitrary order. The long loop equal the `filter` function and will not change original `os.listdir` order. That's why `get_files_to_delete` returns *random* names instead of eldest.

Testing `reverse` flag tells, that without it the eldest file names are at the top of `result` list, but files to delete are slice from the tail. And when the `reverse` flag is enabled, the newest file names are on the top of `result` list, that's why they are protected from deletion.

The only case where it won't be identical between them all is that half-way through the Pex invocations a new version of coverage is released to PyPI. With `coverage>=5.0.3,<5.1`, Pex/Pip will always resolve the newest release that is >= 5.03 and < 5.1. So, there is a possible race condition where one Pex invocation resolves 5.0.3 and then a couple seconds later 5.0.4 gets released to PyPI and the next invocation resolves that.

(Although, actually, I don't think that race condition can even occur due to V2 caching. We will cache the result of the EPR used to create the coverage Pex. So long as the inputs to the rule—such as the `--coverage-version` string—don't change, we won't try re-resolving  the Pex.)

@lissyx we have too many checkpoint loading cases:

|From|To|Transfer|
|---|---|---|
|CuDNN|CuDNN|No|
|CuDNN|CuDNN|Yes|
|CuDNN|LSTMBlockFusedCell|No|
|CuDNN|LSTMBlockFusedCell|Yes|
|LSTMBlockFusedCell|LSTMBlockFusedCell|No|
|LSTMBlockFusedCell|LSTMBlockFusedCell|Yes|
|LSTMBlockFusedCell|CuDNN|No|
|LSTMBlockFusedCell|CuDNN|Yes|

I don't even know if some of these cases work before or after this PR, as I've never tried them (loading a LSTMBlockFusedCell checkpoint onto a CuDNN graph).

The way I see it, the motivation for the separate `load` and `save` flags is that some of these operations are seen as "more lossy" than others, or at least more dangerous. There are three general operations:

1. Continuing a run
2. Transfer learning from a checkpoint
3. Convert a CuDNN checkpoint to LSTMBlockFusedCell (or vice-versa)

I'd say most users have different expectations of data loss when doing operation 1 compared to 2 and 3. I transfer/convert something from X to Y, there's an expectation of a source and a destination even in the language used to describe these operations.

Users are already hitting problems here like mixing up CuDNN and LSTMBlockFusedCell, so I'd say it's pretty much guaranteed the transfer learning is going to cause people to get confused. But I don't think keeping it to one flag is going to avoid that. I think the benefit of being lossless in all causes outweighs the added complexity of having to specify separate load and save dirs for common cases.

`\*` down in the docstring below is an invalid escape character, and is technically deprecated since Python 3.6. Sometimes people escape `**kwargs` in docstrings with `\*\*kwargs` (same thing with `*args` -> `\*args`, thinking that sphinx will start bold/emphasis formatting. But sphinx actually handles this, and suggests omitting the backslashes for varargs: https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/extensions/example_numpy.html

So, **TL;DR**, don't use `r"""` docstrings containing `\*\*kwargs`, use plain unicode `"""` docstrings containing `**kwargs`.

So on second thought, perhaps this is just an extension to the config API. Like we can just parse out the dictionary separately just as you described, but also provide a thin wrapper that implements a functional analog to 
`partial(func, **dict(get_default_args(func), **updates))` 
just with all of the fancy command line stuff and whatnot.

config.load_dataset = config_function(dataset_loader) 
config.load_dataset.b = 6
config.load_dataset.n = 7

...
config.load_dataset(5, n=2) # [30, 30]
```

This was the message that caused the most trouble for me. 


This first issue was in getting rid of the
```
- tried `array<WorkflowStep>` but
Expected a list, was <class 'int'>
```
message. 

In `ArrayLoader` objects of the array are loaded like,
```
lf = load_field(
                	doc[i], _UnionLoader(([self, self.items])), baseuri, loadingOptions, lc=lc
            	)
```
Which means an array of WorkflowStep will try loading the step with `array<WorkflowStep>` as well as `WorkflowStep`. This is what causes the messaging.

I first tried just having the loader be like

```
lf = load_field(
                	doc[i], self.items, baseuri, loadingOptions, lc=lc
            	)
```
but this caused an issue that certain fields weren't being passed through UnionLoader that were supposed to be, and "checking object {obj}" with lc info wasn't in error messages.

I then tried
```
lf = load_field(
                	doc[i], UnionLoader(self.items), baseuri, loadingOptions, lc=lc
            	)
```
which worked perfectly for all the test cases, but caused metaschema tests to fail.

Other similar changes (trying to suppress array error messages for example) cause downstream consequences

The approach I came up with that seemed to work with minimal changes, is in `UnionLoader`, if `t.alternates` includes something like `array<WorkflowStep>` as well as other items, suppress the messages coming from `array<WorkflowStep>`.

The other issue I had was with the generic object vs specific object type.

I was able to get error message 5 to read
```

as we know, there cannot be 2 duplictated keys in Python dict. So the filter could be either {"tag1": "val1"} or {"tag1": "val2"}, no matter which case, the len(matched_tags) will be 1, which matchs len(filters)

We have had a problem with overly specific mocks in the past and prefer using moto where possible for mocking AWS api calls.

For example this would become:

https://github.com/spulec/moto/blob/master/tests/test_iam/test_iam.py#L153-L167
```
    @mock_iam
    def test_expand_role(self): 
         conn = boto3.client('iam', region_name='us-east-1')
         conn.create_role(RoleName="test-role", AssumeRolePolicyDocument="some policy")

         hook = SageMakerHook(aws_conn_id='sagemaker_test_conn_id') 
         arn = hook.expand_role(some_role)
         self.assertEqual("arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/test-role")
```

This way it still goes through all the Boto3 client-side validation etc, which has caught some bugs for us in the past.

We should use this similarly for S3 operations, but I don't think moto supports SageMaker yet, so we'd still have to mock those API calls.

The historic spec is still enforced by the solver, it will not be removed.  The addition of the target [moves the package](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.7.5/conda/resolve.py#L1191-L1204) from the "requested packages" group to the "all other packages" group.  The requested packages group is [optimized early](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.7.5/conda/resolve.py#L1213-L1218) where as all other packages group is [optimized later](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.7.5/conda/resolve.py#L1260-L1269).

With the old code if you can't freeze the historic spec was considered at the same level as the explicit specs, so `conda install anaconda` followed by `conda update ipython` would optimize both the version numbers of `anaconda` and `ipython`.

The current code keeps the historic spec but the explicit specs are given priority.  In the `anaconda` and `ipython` example `conda update ipython` will optimize the version of `ipython` with a constraint that some `anaconda` version must be installed.

This new behavior is in-line with how historic and explicit specs were considered in conda 4.6.

Naming is unfortunate and inconsistent. Note that each user will need to write following names at least once somewhere:
```
method:              divide_logs_by_result
named param:         div_by_res_logdir
named param:         div_by_res_subdirs
default subdir name: div_by_res
```
Let's unpack this. 
1. Division of logs is not the goal, having them laid-out/filtered/sorted by outcome is closer to what user wants. I assume `by_outcome` is more accurate than `div`/`division`, especially since `outcome` is the term which pytest uses  for `failed`/`passed`/`skipped` set.
2. It makes little sense to use 3/4-word identifiers.
3. It's good to take scoping into account - named param can have almost any name (except keywords or builtins) without fear of colliding with anything, so `bla_bla_bla_foodir` is superfluous, use `foodir`.
4. It's not logdir, logdir is the thing called `logs` by default. It's the logdir's subdir. So let's call it subdir.
5. I think user would want to choose outcomes more frequently than the name of subdir, let's change the order of arguments.

I'd propose this:
```
def split_by_outcome(self, outcomes=['failed'], subdir='by_outcome')
```
which can be used this way:
```
logger_config.split_by_outcome()
logger_config.split_by_outcome(['failed', 'passed'])
logger_config.split_by_outcome(['failed', 'passed'], 'some_other_name')
logger_config.split_by_outcome(subdir='some_other_name')
```

So the standard requires one default behaviour for databases with transactions support and another one for databases without it. It becomes a problem if a database did not support transactions, but than implemented them: the default behaviour must be changed and it is incompatible change. I propose to do our best:

* Provide `autocommit` connection property with default value `True`. An attempt to set the property to `False` should raise NotSupportedError, but it may be set to `True`. Also it worth to raise an InterfaceError on attempt to set the property to a non-boolean value or `None`.
* Suggest to verify or explicitly set the property to `True` in code that rely on the autocommit mode behaviour.

I'll include the following paragraph to the Databse API wiki page (not created yet) and to future release notes:

> The module enables autocommit mode by default, which is reflected in the `<connection object>.autocommit` property. This default will be changed when [interactive transactions support](https://github.com/tarantool/tarantool/issues/2016) will land into tarantool and will implemented in the module (it is PEP 249 requirement). Code that rely on the autocommit mode should either verify the property or explicitly set it to `True`.

Unless the information loss is pretty dramatic, it seems reasonable to me to say that this falls on the user: if you really think you need to maintain fine-grained resolution, you can wait an hour for your design matrices. My intention is to help share our experience from integrating and deploying Thread in different Nest/Google products 
and point out which configs can be useful in different scenarios.

> seems this then become a question of how much un-acked traffic is travelling off the mesh. That's the only case that would create a potentially negative scenario from having the cache_snoop entries higher (i don't believe any of this matters if devices on mesh < max_cache).

Note that frames are acked at every hop at Mac layer. If a frame is sent across multiple hops over Thread mesh to a BR, the BR will not sent an ack to the original sender.

As I said, Thread network should behave fine even if snoop optimization is not used (i.e., setting the `MAX_SNOOP_ENTRIES` to zero). Using the snoop optimization will help reduce the delay in the (first) interaction (request response exchange) originating from a Thread device to an off-mesh entity (server). With snoop optimization when BR receives the response from server to forward within mesh, it does not need to perform address query for the mesh destination and can use the snooped cache entry.

Yeah, I went back and forth here between these two options. I didn't firmly settle on a Series being the best option, but I leaned that way because the downstream uses are going to be very different depending on whether `stat_fn` returns a scalar or a dict:

* If it returns a scalar then presumably each branch is going to be represented by a scalar, and the data will be presented as a bar chart or similar, with one bar per branch.
* If it returns a dict then presumably each branch is going to be represented by a list of numbers, and the data will be presented as a line or scatter plot, with one series per branch.

I'm also a bit uncomfortable with the fact that you can't tell whether `stat_fn` returns a scalar or dict without executing it. I toyed with the idea of having separate `compare_branches` functions for scalar and multivalued `stat_fn`s, since they really are a separate style of analysis.

So I don't think that there exists a "generic" downstream use, and "tripping" might be a good thing (the closest we get to type safety? haha). Does this change your view? Maybe (as the representative of the downstream user!) you have viable plans in mind for which my concern does not apply?

We now interpret both the DetailedDiff keys and the `replaceOnChanges` items as paths, and then determine whether a replaceOnChange path "contains" each detailed diff key. Despite it being the current behavior, you could certainly make an argument that these warnings shouldn't be displayed when using best_effort in quiet mode.

I think for me what feels like "correct" behavior depends on how you're using `--allow-subset-of-names`. The initial motivation behind the flag was damage mitigation. If you're running a server hosting multiple domains and you're failing challenges for some reason, obtain an updated cert for as many domains as possible rather than letting it expire for all domains in the cert.

If you're using the flag in this way, I think I'd expect to be clearly told when domains are being dropped. If, however, you're using it because you want to remove a domain from a cert, the warning is probably unnecessary. (I might argue that doing the latter is a bad way to do things though because you may drop more domains than you expect. In this case, explicitly saying what domains were dropped may still be helpful).

I'm certainly biased, but I personally prefer we continue to print the warning when using `--allow-subset-of-names`. I'm not that worried about it though so if you disagree, we can leave it out.

The diff here looks bigger than the actual change. Here are some points on what is changed:
- The `while()` iteration loop is changed to a ` for()`: this allows us to use `continue` to skip over entries.
- We move up the check for entries that are expired and use `continue` to skip over next entry in loop if no need to retx the entry.
- In the case that retx is required for an expired entry, the `mNextTimerShot` gets updated.
- The code which updates the next timer fire time (`nextTime` variable) is now moved at the end and is common for both cases (where entry is not expired or where the entry is expired and we need a retx).
- After the loop, we check if the we need to start the timer with ` nextTime`.

I don't think "highly unlikely" is a good argument. It will someday fail and confuse someone. Tests should be deterministic.

By the way, because the tests should be deterministic, I don't think that just connecting to with the default parameters is a good idea - the tests will depend on environment that they run in, and what `bitcoin.conf` is there is unpredictable. I would introduce a special environment variable with unique prefix, like `BITCOINLIB_TEST_RUN_ENABLE_RPC_CONF=...some_test_bitcoin.conf` or something like that, that would indicate that the environment is ready for the tests, and should use a special config. This way an automatic test service like travis ci can be used to setup a test environment where RPC can be tested, but the tests won't have any unexpected effects in other environments.

In general, user would not expect that some tests could even connect to their working bitcoind, for example, and may not be happy about that even if the commands are innoulous. On the other hand, the connection might block if the daemon port is filtered via dropping the packets rather than sending RST back, and then the test will be stuck for quite a long time.

The `error_cls` variable only exists in the `for ...` loop above. We might need to change the error-handling and retry logic overall here:

- We retry a task, only if it fails for a specific set of exceptions (defined in a `GITHUB_RELEASE_RETRY_EXCEPTIONS` config variable). When that happens, we set the `release.model.error` message to something like "Release failed for an unknown reason; automatic retry scheduled."
- Otherwise, we allow the default handler to handle the error message (and no retry takes place, since e.g. if the `.zenodo.json` metadata is invalid, this is something that a retry won't help with
- After the maximum number of retries, we also allow the exception handler to set the error (if you check in the Celery docs, there is a special `RetryError` exception raised when you call `.retry()` after the maximum number of retries is performed)

Changing an attribute of a subtype is not reflected on the parent containing that subtype, because _retrieving_ a subtype returns a _new_ subtype instance (constructed from parent's `_properties`). As I read it, when gromgull migrated the SPARQL paths processor `paths.py` out of the sparql module back in 2103, the change from monkeypatching Path to directly defining Path methods [definitions](https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/43d86224382d64fba2e5e17acedea1704631e097/rdflib/paths.py#L199) overrode the `|` (`__or__` and `__ror__`)  infixOwl delimiters causing exceptions in the infixOwl doctests. Hence my editing the docstrings to use the existing alternative implementation of `<<` and `>>` (`__ rshift` and `__rlshift__`) instead.

I subsequently discovered that unfortunately, SPARQL* uses the Turtle* double-anglebrackets and, anticipating that addition to `Path`, I tried to find an alternative to use with infixOwl. As far as I could ascertain, matmul was the only remaining option but yes, I guess it will  clash with `@` as lang tag so there doesn't appear to be any ready solution for enabling infixOwl to work in RDFLib with the “colloquial programming style” described in the infixOwl docstrings. 

>  think this Infix OWL has diverged from the spec somewhat

TBH, those specific infix operators aren't part of the spec, they were added by Chimezie as part of FuXi and weren't in the RDFLib 2.4 of the time. I'm not going to spend time arguing for the retention of infixOwl if the implementation has aged to a degree that it's now basically out of scope, other than to observe the irony of Harold (funowl) Solbrig's [respect for it](https://github.com/hsolbrig/funowl#other-packages).

In case the logic of eliminating this nannying isn't clear here: the resolver (Pip) has solved the requirements successfully at this point. That means if there are dup requirements, one of two situations is in play:
1. The requirements were compatible. An example is ["PyJWT", "PyJWT==1.7.1"].
2. The requirements were incompatible but only 1 was in-play, so ... they were compatible - everything taken into account: ["PyJWT==1.7.1; python_version == '2.7.*'", "PyJWT>1.7.1; python_version >= '3.6'"]

As such, there is no need to second guess. In case 1, where there are 2 requirements, the runtime `sys.path` assembly logic will necessarily find the one PyJWT distribution that was resolved satisfies both requirements. In case 2, the runtime `sys.path` assembly logic will find only one of the two requirements is active per `Marker.evaluate()`.

```bash
# Create a podman container with "-w"
podman create --name=foobar-podman-test --interactive -w "/createme-dir" python:3.7 /bin/bash

# Running the exec here will fail because the container is not started
podman exec -i foobar-podman-test mkdir -p "/createme-dir"

# But starting the container raises an error when create was used with "-w"
podman start foobar-podman-test

# Cleanup
podman rm --force -v foobar-podman-test
```

```bash
# Demo where this sequence works with docker
docker create --name=foobar-docker-test --interactive -w "/createme-dir"  python:3.7 /bin/bash
docker start foobar-docker-test
docker exec -i foobar-docker-test mkdir -p "/createme-dir"
docker rm --force -v foobar-docker-test
```

Regarding why we might want to allow `column_names` to be a Callable, I had the idea that it helps support being explicit over implicit, which is in the Zen of Python. Setting `column_names=None` makes selecting all columns implicit, whereas setting `column_names=lambda df: df.columns` makes selecting all columns explicit. In addition, it allows the selection of arbitrary subsets of column names programmatically, without needing to hard-code those names.

On further thought, I can see how `column_names=None` actually follows the pattern established in other places in the library, so I think, for now, we can:

1. Use `column_names=None` to imply selection of all columns, and
2. Talk more about `column_names: Callable` in the issue tracker, deferring the implementation till later.

What do you all think about `jointly=True` as the keyword for triggering whether to _independently_ scale each column or to _jointly_ scale all columns specified in `column_names`? @Zeroto521 if you're in agreement with the keyword argument, then I think, let's get that specified in this PR, then we can close out the PR!

all right.. I dug deep into it and found more pitfalls in the **annotation** part and finally got them resolved.

There're several findings/problems in our previous implementation:

1) out-going messages can't get what it sets in the annotation due to asymmetrical key of setter/getter.
  - We wrap bytes key into `AMQP.SymbolValue` key to create entry in the annotation dict.
  - We use bytes key to retrieve info from the annotation dict (that's what amqp library gives back).
2) every time we set key-pair in annotation, we create a new entry instead of replacing the old one, which leads to buggy behavior.
3) we can't set scheduled_enqueue_time_utc to None, it leads to different bugs on different platforms.
4) The `AMQP.SymbolValue` key is a must for `scheduled_enqueue_time_utc` if we want to schedule message, while for `partition_key` and `via_partition_key` in the annotation, either key being type of `AMQP.SymbolValue` or bytes works.

Now I've made getter/setter on the annotation property consistent (partition_key, via_partition_key, scheduled_enqueue_time_utc consistent).

> This way the script can just be started without installing a development environment.

I agree that's a nice property in theory, but wonder how much it really buys us in practice. If a dev wants to run it locally, I think it's reasonable to require and expect them to already have the development environment set up. For CI, I think the performance overhead of running `tools/pip_install.py tabulate` is very minimal. You wouldn't have to even create a venv if you want even though it only takes a couple seconds to do so. In both cases, I think the import error that would be raised automatically if `tabulate` doesn't exist is pretty explanatory.

Did you have other use cases in mind for this script where not having any third party dependencies is a significant benefit? If not, I'd prefer to add a relatively popular dependency that allows us to make arbitrary, nicely formatted tables in just a couple lines of code.

If you do have a use case or you're not convinced in general, feel free to take a look at format options. The main thing I was thinking there is I think snippets like:
```
{0}'.format(states[arch] + ' ' * (27 - len(states[arch]))) for arch in archs)))
```
can become:
```
{0:<27}'.format(states[arch] for arch in archs)))
```
You could also maybe look into setting the cell width in a variable and reusing it everywhere, possibly by making use of [f-strings](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0498/) to simplify things, but that may be more trouble than it is worth.

> I think this might be better > > `(§§?)|[Ss](ec)?(tions?)?\.?` > > the one you suggested . I think would capture weird anomalies like `S§.` How about this? `(?:\s§§|§|[Ss](?:ec)(?:tion)?s?\.?\s)` The one you suggested would match S or s whenever it appears alone or in a word (e.g. "case" matches). Making "ec" required solves this, but it will still match words that include sec or section (e.g. "intersect" or "Sectarian"), which is why I required the match to be preceded and succeeded by whitespace. We could use lookaheads and lookbehinds for this, but it would slow things down and, if the plan is to use the section_marker in many of the law citations, the speed may be significant. Yours also won't match the plural Secs. because you made the s option in "tions". Finally, I made the internal groups non-capturing in case anybody has a use case in which the pull matches by iterators/position. If we are using the section_marker, people will not be able to look at the regex to see how many match groups it creates in laws.json, so it could really throw them off.

It's not a temporary or managed checkpoint. It's just a checkpoint.

If you deconstruct a dict checkpoint, the memory is freed. If you deconstruct this file checkpoint, the disk is freed. If you send it somewhere else first, it may or may not be persisted (same with dict checkpoints).

We should not think of Checkpoints as being different depending on their internal data representation. When I send a Checkpoint via the object store, it's actually a bytes checkpoint on deserialization. We don't care as long as we can convert it into a processable format with to_dict() or to_directory().

The fact that this checkpoint is actually stored on disk is an implementation detail. It could also live in memory.

We are schematizing the default service names for integrations here.  In othe words, manual spans and manually specified overrides still take precedence.

The priority order after this change is (generally, not in all cases):

1. Manual service name
2. DD_INTEGRATION_SERVICE env var
3. DD_INTEGRATION_SERVICE_NAME env var
4. DD_SERVICE
5. "unnamed-python-service"

If any level evaluates to "False" in an assert statement, then we go down one.

Alternatively, you could lean in on using the sizes of values to decide what your target size is. This could be a really tricky one. KMP_AFFINITY default value has some conflict with onnxruntime's core resource control (which makes it behave strangely). We cannot unset or reset this sys variable once the user start their python script. Some solution we have used in training is creating a new process(with different KMP_AFFINITY) to handle the work, while it is not reasonable as well since creating a process will cost 1-10ms and very unfriendly to low latency requirement. 

One possible solution is:
1. make `KMP_AFFINITY` default value be None(that is not setting this value in `bigdl-nano-init`)
2. Only set this value during multi-process training by creating new processes (since this var does not give much postive affect for inferencing or single process training)

Also I'm not sure what your preference is but I do not like putting spaces in regex. I prefer to use the whitespace command (\s) in case there is a nonbreaking space or something similar, and I usually put * or + so that multiple spaces match and, in the former case, a missing space will also match. For example:
```
(?P<title>\d+)\s+$reporter\s+(?:§|[Ss](ec)?(tion)?)[§|s]?\.?\s*$law_section
```
requires some whitespace between the title and the reporter name and between the reporter name and the section identifier, but it merely permits white space between the section identifier and the law_section. This means §1 will match.

>>> a1 = np.arange(5)

2.2 µs ± 120 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100000 loops each)

Actually, even the correction is wrong. It should be the following instead:

```diff
+You can put the ``@nonreentrant(<key>)`` decorator on a ``__default__`` function; but we recommend against it, because in most circumstances it will not work as intended.
```
Rationality: 

- `You can put the ``@nonreentrant(<key>)`` decorator on a ``__default__`` function;` is an independent clause, and leads to a comma splice error. To avoid this, we add a `;`
- `because in most circumstances it will not work as intended.` is a relative clause which adds to the first part (or `but we recommend against it,` so we add a comma here).
- As for the change to `as intended` -- it is so for concision.

Imagine you have two rollout workers and one of them has a policy that has undergone 2 updates thus far, the other uses a policy that has undergone 3 updates thus far. Both workers produce rollouts of length 50 (rollout_fragment_length==50). The train batch size is 100, so we concatenate these 2x len=50 batches to one that's 100 ts long. This new 100ts batch will have its `num_gradient_updates` property set to 2.5 as it's the weighted average (both original batches contribute 50%).

Well, it's no big deal, but the reason why I suggested the change is that `validate.text` is just an alias for the actual `str` type and it executes the same singledispatch validation method `validate_type` as other base types like `int`. `text` a left-over module export of the py2 compatibility era, and removing it would've required changing all validation schemas at that time, which didn't make much sense, so it was left.
- https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/commit/c94a8107916a284e69c8c14dda3894d9f8d81495#diff-c415fd588e79ab1a5ef3a0fc4fbf7a90769778435b75a80b589e9c7b02afcbd7R37
- https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/37ee5347508980ee768ac0695714f01c54600933/src/streamlink/plugin/api/validate.py#L427-L436

All newly added or edited plugins are using the base types in their validation schemas.

Yes, the issue is, the ID will always be lowercase when part of the host. That's why I thought we should force the restApiId to be lower case, even though the user specified a cased tag. (tag could be cased, but not the ID once used to create the restApi). 
Even though it's a LocalStack feature, a regular restApiId is always lowercase, and AWS will fail if you upper case an existing ID. Making it case insensitive changes that behaviour.

We could also simply raise an error if the user provided tag value is not lowercase for `__custom_id__` or something. 
My general idea is just that `restApiId` should be lowercase. There are no changes yet in behaviour anyway, as I keep it in a `CaseInsentiveDict`, but it makes the lookup more complicated for no real gain on our side.

> That seems like a limitation of mypy.

Yes. But there’s a good reason for this limitation, since it’s a consequence of the more important Liskov substitution principle: wherever an expression of type `T` is expected, it should be type-correct to provide a value whose type is a subclass of `T`.

Remember, every non-iterable type might be subclassed by an iterable type (this is not an uncommon corner case: *every* iterable type is a subclass of `object`). So if there were a hypothetical syntax that purported to guarantee that `always_iterable(obj): T` when `obj: T` and `T` is non-iterable, one could assign an instance of an iterable subclass `U` to `obj: T` and get back a result from `always_iterable(obj)` that breaks this guarantee.

A simpler way to say this is that there’s *no such thing* as a non-iterable type, provided that one recognizes a type to implicitly include all of its subclasses. Giving up that provision would have unpleasant ripple effects on every other aspect of the type system (I think you would basically need to augment each type annotation with a flag saying whether or not a subclass is allowed).

> I notice that always_iterable does already have a type specification.

`Any` is never a type specification; it just disables type checking for that value and every value derived from it. (See the [mypy documentation](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/dynamic_typing.html) and [avoid the `Any` type](https://zulip.readthedocs.io/en/latest/testing/mypy.html#avoid-the-any-type).) Given that type checking is finding real bugs, we should be very hesitant to disable it for the sake of stylistic preferences.

There will always be some cases where appeasing the type checker rather than disabling it will mean writing code in a different way than you might have otherwise. No static type system can be powerful enough to capture all the ways a language as dynamic as Python can be used. I acknowledge that this is unfortunate. But I hope you’ll find as I have that the benefits of full type checking outweigh these inconveniences.

> I think the same could be said for `or []`, which is also fragile based on the value of `ob.__bool__`.

It’s conventional in Python, to the point of being enshrined in PEP 8, that testing a sequence as a boolean is an idiomatic check for emptiness. But let’s consider all the corner cases:

* A sequence that’s truthy but empty is fine since the result of `or []` is still empty.
* A sequence that’s falsy but nonempty would be treated as empty—given the PEP 8 idiom, if you write such a sequence, you should have expected that some users will treat it that way—but this doesn’t break type safety.
* There’s no value that’s `None` but truthy, since `NoneType` cannot be subclassed.

If that doesn’t satisfy you, here are some alternatives that type check:

> Microsoft Compiler used to generate strange code that allocates two registers and slowdowns nearby code.

The godbolt output you linked shows that MSVC does basically the same - replaces `mfence` with `lock cmpxchg`. Yes, `cmpxchg` is not the best replacement, but I would still expect it to be far better than `mfence`, even with two clobbered registers. See [here](https://uops.info/table.html?search=lock%20cmpxchg&cb_lat=on&cb_tp=on&cb_uops=on&cb_ports=on&cb_SKL=on&cb_ICL=on&cb_ZEN3=on&cb_measurements=on&cb_doc=on&cb_base=on&cb_sse=on) and [here](https://uops.info/table.html?search=mfence&cb_lat=on&cb_tp=on&cb_uops=on&cb_ports=on&cb_SKL=on&cb_ICL=on&cb_ZEN3=on&cb_measurements=on&cb_doc=on&cb_base=on&cb_sse=on) for performance numbers. The bottom line is `mfence` is very slow (about half the throughput), after all security mitigations, so replacing it with a `lock`-prefixed operation is a win.

I can leave the `mfence` in place if you insist, I just don't see the point.

> GCC 11 generates really good code, so the proposed patch is going to be outdated.

Yes, gcc 11 have optimized the thread fence intrinsics. It uses `lock or [rsp], 0` as a replacement though, which might be less efficient than the asm statement I proposed because:

- `or` instruction affects flags,
- `[rsp]` is not necessarily the location of the latest accessed data on the stack, as the compiler tends to allocate the stack frame beforehand. This means that `[rsp]` is less likely to be in the cache than a variable that is inside the stack frame, near the latest allocated variable in the preceding code.

I cannot say how much these nuances affect performance, probably not by much. I can limit the asm code to gcc versions prior to 11 if you prefer.

> If you think that the patch is useful for your use case, I would refactor out `order` argument

Sure, I can do that, though I'd prefer to remove `order` in a separate commit.

While I'm advancing with the draft implementation of this logic, I'd like to propose a hierarchy of "safeness" for modules to be referred to when saving by reference. If an object is found in multiple modules, the safest alternative would always be preferred, as it has more chances to be there at unpickling (it also applies to `refimported`).

The proposed hierarchy is:
1. Standard Library modules
2. modules of the same package as the module being saved
3. installed modules in general ("builtin" modules)
4. non-installed modules ("user" modules)
5. `__main__` — never safe

Cases 1 to 3 would fall under the "builtin" umbrella of the table from my previous comment.

```python
output = ""
current_width = 0
iterator = iter_parse(self._term, self)
for text, cap in iterator:
    if not cap:
        current_width += wcwidth(text)
        if current_width > width:
            break
    output += text
for text, cap in iterator:
    if cap:
        output += text
return output
```

The reason this works is because we save the generator (and its state) so we can iterate over it with a different algorithm depending. That's what the `break` statement there does.

First we simply go through each element in the text, if it's printable we check the width. If the width is okay, we add it.

If the width is not okay, we break out of the first iteration loop and proceed to the second. In the second we simply check if it's a control sequence, since we know for sure we never want any more text after that, and we can skip the `wcwidth` call.

In the worst case, where the entire text fits into the width after all, the generator is already fully exhausted, so tthe second loop is skipped naturally.
